coredata.dc:description: Creatine (CR) is an active compound closely associated with meat quality. However, current techniques for measuring CR in meat face challenges in achieving rapid and low-cost detection. In this study, nanozyme based on iron-doped carbon dots (Fe-LCDs) was synthesized by employing sodium lignosulfonate and iron as the precursors. Iron incorporation was crucial for significantly enhancing the peroxidase-like activity of Fe-LCDs. A cascade sensing platform was developed by integrating creatinase (CRH) and sarcosine oxidase (SOx) with Fe-LCDs. In this system, CR interacts with the catalytic framework to generate superoxide anions, which catalyzes the oxidation of 3,3′,5,5’-Tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) to produce a blue-colored product, enabling colorimetric detection of CR. Furthermore, a sensing platform compatible with smartphones was established, successfully facilitating rapid, sensitive, and visual detection of CR in meat. This research not only synthesized an effective CDs-based nanozyme but also devised a novel and promising nanoparticle-based sensing platform for CR detection.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of Fe-LCDs. (a) TEM images (inset: HR-TEM) and (b) particle size distribution of Fe-LCDs; (c) FT-IR spectrum of Fe-LCDs; (d) XPS full spectrum and high-resolution XPS spectra of (e) Fe 2p and (f) Cl 2p of Fe-LCDs.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of different reaction systems of TMB, TMB + H2O2, TMB + Fe-LCDs, and TMB + H2O2 + Fe-LCDs. Insets: corresponding digital photographs; (b) UV–vis absorption spectra for LCDs group reacted with 50 mM H2O2 and Fe-LCDs group reacted with 5 μM H2O2. Insets: corresponding digital photographs; Effect of (c) pH and (d) temperature on peroxidase-like activity of Fe-LCDs; (e) Absorbance intensity at 652 nm for Fe-LCDs system treated with His (L-histidine), IPA (isopropanol), and BQ (benzoquinone) scavengers; (f) ESR spectra of O2 ·- in different reaction systems of DMPO + H2O2 and DMPO + H2O2 + Fe-LCDs. Each error bar in (e) represents the standard deviation of three independent measurements. The values in (e) are presented as mean ± SD (n = 3).

Steady-state kinetic studies of Fe-LCDs for (a) TMB and (b) H2O2; The corresponding Lineweaver-Burk double-reciprocal plots of Fe-LCDs for (c) TMB and (d) H2O2; Each error bar represents the standard deviation of three independent measurements. All values are presented as mean ± SD (n = 3).

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of various reaction systems. Insets: corresponding digital photographs; (b) UV–vis absorption spectra of reaction systems containing different concentrations of CR. Insets: corresponding digital photographs; (c) Linear fitting relationship between the absorbance intensity at 652 nm versus the concentrations of CR; (d) Selectivity investigation of Fe-LCDs for CR detection; (e) Catalytic stability evaluation of Fe-LCDs in solid powder forms within 24 weeks; (f) Catalytic stability evaluation of Fe-LCDs in solution forms within 12 days. The values in (c-f) are presented as mean ± SD (n = 3).

(a) Schematic diagram of HSL (Hue, Saturation, and Lightness) extraction processing of colorimetric images for different concentrations of CR; (b) Linear fitting relationship between the relative color information S versus the concentrations of CR; (c) Detection of CR in duck, beef, pork, lamb and chicken from local food markets (referred to as Sample 1 in each group) and restaurants (referred to as Sample 2 in each group) by colorimetric detection, smartphone-assisted sensing platform and UPLC, respectively. The values in (b) and (c) are presented as mean ± SD (n = 3).

Mechanism and application of smartphone-assisted Fe-LCDs nanozyme sensing platform for colorimetric analysis of CR. (a) Synthesis mechanism diagram of Fe-LCDs, illustrating the hydrothermal synthesis process using sodium lignosulfonate and ferric chloride as precursors; (b) Schematic diagram of the detection mechanism of CR by using Fe-LCDs, highlighting the enzyme-nanozyme cascade reaction and the generation of colorimetric signals; (c) Application of Fe-LCDs nanozyme-based colorimetric detection method integrated with the smartphone-assisted sensing platform for detecting CR in meat samples, validated by UPLC.

head.ce:title: Smartphone-assisted colorimetric sensing platform based on Fe-LCDs nanozymes for detection of creatine in meat

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Creatine (CR) is an active compound closely associated with meat quality. However, current techniques for measuring CR in meat face challenges in achieving rapid and low-cost detection. In this study, nanozyme based on iron-doped carbon dots (Fe-LCDs) was synthesized by employing sodium lignosulfonate and iron as the precursors. Iron incorporation was crucial for significantly enhancing the peroxidase-like activity of Fe-LCDs. A cascade sensing platform was developed by integrating creatinase (CRH) and sarcosine oxidase (SOx) with Fe-LCDs. In this system, CR interacts with the catalytic framework to generate superoxide anions, which catalyzes the oxidation of 3,3′,5,5’-Tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) to produce a blue-colored product, enabling colorimetric detection of CR. Furthermore, a sensing platform compatible with smartphones was established, successfully facilitating rapid, sensitive, and visual detection of CR in meat. This research not only synthesized an effective CDs-based nanozyme but also devised a novel and promising nanoparticle-based sensing platform for CR detection.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Meat serves as a critically important source of protein in the human diet (Hurtley & Ash, 2018; Khaled et al., 2021; Li, Wang, et al., 2022). The quality of meat directly influences the lives and health of consumers, making it imperative to maintain high standards of meat quality to accommodate the rising consumer demand and safeguard public health (Zheng et al., 2024). Creatine (CR) is a nitrogenous organic acid that can be endogenously synthesized within the body or obtained from meat-based diets (Brosnan & Brosnan, 2016). By maintaining energy metabolism and pH balance, CR is able to delay the glycogenolysis, proteolysis, and oxidation processes, thereby comprehensively improving the quality of meat (Liu et al., 2021). Consequently, CR can serve as a potential candidate bioactive compound to evaluate meat quality (Elbir & Oz, 2020; Jung et al., 2013). Current analytical techniques for CR detection encompass ultraviolet spectrophotometry (Elbir & Oz, 2020), Raman spectroscopy (Moreira et al., 2019), electrochemical methods (Surucu & Abaci, 2022), capillary electrophoresis (See et al., 2012), high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Feizi et al., 2022), along with other methods. Despite their utility, these methods are encumbered by high costs, lengthy processing times, complex procedures, and the risk of introducing toxic reagents (Polak et al., 2009). Hence, it is crucial to develop a convenient, cost-effective, rapid, and eco-friendly method for determining CR in meat.

Nanozymes, mimicking natural enzymes, offer multifunctionality, tunable activity, affordability, and enhanced enzymic stability (Jiang et al., 2024; Li, Li, et al., 2022; Li, Liu, et al., 2022; Li, Wang, et al., 2022; Mei et al., 2021). Recently, nanozyme-mediated catalytic oxidation reactions have demonstrated promise in colorimetric detection, including chemical sensing, biomedicine, environmental protection, and food testing (Gong et al., 2017; Wang, Chen, & Zhao, 2023; Zhang et al., 2022). According to previous studies, the detection of creatinine can be realized via an enzyme-nanozyme cascade sensing platform. In this platform, creatinine is sequentially oxidized by creatininase (CAH), creatinase (CRH), and sarcosine oxidase (SOx) to produce hydrogen peroxide (H₂O₂). Subsequently, the peroxidase nanozymes catalyze H₂O₂ and promote the oxidation of the substrate 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), thereby enabling colorimetric sensing of creatinine (on behalf of the Ifcc Scientific Di, I. F. o. C, 2008; Zhu et al., 2022). Inspired by previous contributions, it is feasible to establish a viable approach for detecting CR in meat by combining the enzyme cascade with nanozyme-catalyzed colorimetric reactions. Smartphone-assisted colorimetric technologies, equipped with robust image and data processing capabilities, as well as the capacity for immediate data capture, preservation, and dissemination, have ushered in a novel era for portable detection methods (Huang et al., 2024; Yuan et al., 2025; Zhang et al., 2025). Therefore, integrating the enzyme-nanozyme cascade sensing platform with smartphones is highly significant for establishing an intelligent detection system to achieve the sensitive, accurate, and convenient detection of CR.

Among nanozyme materials, carbon dots (CDs) are particularly noteworthy due to their nanoscale size, minimal toxicity, robust stability, and excellent water solubility (Liu, Qiao, et al., 2022; Liu, Xu, et al., 2022; Lopez-Cantu et al., 2022; Wang, Chen, & Zhao, 2023; Wang, Sun, et al., 2023; Wang, Zhang, et al., 2023). Sodium lignosulfonate is a renewable and environmentally friendly material that is used to synthesize CDs-based peroxidase-like nanozymes. During hydrothermal reactions, its oxygen-containing functional groups oxidize to form carbonyl (C=O) and carboxyl (COOH) groups, which contribute to the peroxidase-like activity of the nanozymes (Xu et al., 2022). Nevertheless, there is still significant potential for augmenting the enzymatic activity of these nanozymes, thereby improving their catalytic kinetics and enhancing their efficacy across a diverse array of applications. Additionally, while single-atom nanozymes derived from sodium lignosulfonate exhibit satisfactory enzyme activity, their synthesis is complex and cumbersome, hindering their development and application (Wang, Sun, et al., 2023). Therefore, there is a pressing need for further systematic exploration into the simple and rational design and engineering of sodium lignosulfonate-based nanozymes.

To overcome the above challenges, we developed a novel colorimetric sensing system for the fast detection of CR based on iron-doped carbon dots (Fe-LCDs), synthesized through a simple and eco-friendly one-pot hydrothermal method with sodium lignosulfonate and ferric chloride as raw materials, as illustrated in Scheme 1 . Unlike previous approaches, our system uniquely integrates CRH and SOx with Fe-LCDs to establish an enzyme-nanozyme cascade sensing platform. The catalytic mechanism reveals that CR is sequentially oxidized by CRH and SOx to generate H₂O₂. Subsequently, Fe-LCDs catalyze the conversion of H₂O₂ into superoxide anions (O2 ·-), which in turn catalyze the transformation of TMB into blue oxidized TMB (oxTMB). To enhance practical applicability, we coupled the Fe-LCDs colorimetric sensing platform with smartphones to construct an integrated sensor for the rapid, sensitive, and accurate visual detection of CR in meat samples. By featuring distinct advantages, including low detection limits, cost-effectiveness, operational simplicity, and visual readouts, we anticipate that this platform will offer a superior alternative to conventional methods for meat quality assessment. This work not only advances the rational design of high-activity nanozymes but also paves the way for transformative applications in consumer meat quality monitoring.

Sodium lignosulfonate (S140863), ferric chloride (FeCl3, 99.9 %, I112064), isopropanol (IPA, 99.9 %, I112016), L-histidine (His, 99.5 %, L755708), creatinase (CRH, 6 U/mg, BR1012), sarcosine oxidase (SOx, 15 U/mg, BR1013), trichloroacetic acid (99 %, T104257), ammonium acetate (99 %, A112057) and sodium hydroxide (NaOH, 96 %, S111498) were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. o-Phenylenediamine (OPD, 99 %, 91452B) and benzoquinone (BQ, 99 %, 12960B) were obtained from Shanghai Adamas Reagent Co., Ltd. Creatine (CR, 95 %, BD122358) was supplied by Shanghai Bid Health Technology Co., Ltd. 3,3′,5,5’-Tetramethylbenzidine (TMB, 98 %, B010071) was purchased from Anhui Zesheng Technology Co., Ltd. Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30 %, 10,011,218) was obtained from China National Pharmaceutical Group Chemical Reagents Co., Ltd. 2,2′-Azino-bis(3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid) (ABTS, 98 %, 01376032) was sourced from Sigma-Aldrich Corporation. Acetonitrile was purchased from Merck&Co., Ltd. in the United States. The meat samples used in our experimental trials were all obtained from distinct individual animals of the same species. To ensure sample diversity and representativeness, these samples were procured from multiple local food markets and restaurants. All chemical reagents used in this study were of analytical grade, and ultrapure water was used for all measurements.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained using a transmission electron microscope (JEOL, JEM-2100, Japan). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded with a spectrometer (PerkinElmer, Spectrum 2, USA) using the potassium bromide pellet method. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data were obtained using an XPS spectrometer (Thermo Scientific, Nexsa, USA). Free radicals were detected using an electron spin resonance (ESR) spectrometer (Bruker, EMXplus, Germany). Concentration measurements were performed by an ultra-high performance liquid chromatography triple quadrupole mass spectrometer (UPLC-MS, Waters, TQS-Cronos, USA). Ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were acquired using a spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, UV-2600i, Japan). Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra were recorded using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, F-7100, Japan).

First, sodium lignosulfonate (0.1 g) and anhydrous ferric chloride (0.1 g) were dissolved in 20 mL of ultrapure water and stirred thoroughly. The resulting pretreatment solution was then transferred to a high-pressure reactor and heated at 180 °C for 12 h (Zhang et al., 2022). Upon completion of the reaction, the solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min to remove large particles and a black suspension was obtained. The supernatant was then filtered through a 0.22 μm needle filter to yield a yellow solution. Following filtration, the solution was placed into a dialysis bag with a retention threshold of 500 Da and dialyzed in ultrapure water for 24 h. Finally, the solution in the dialysis bag was freeze-dried to obtain the final Fe-LCDs.

The peroxidase-like activities of Fe-LCDs were evaluated using TMB, with hydrogen peroxide H2O2 serving as the electron acceptor for the peroxide nanozymes (He et al., 2023). Briefly, Fe-LCDs were dissolved in a HAc/NaAc buffer (0.2 M, pH = 3.5). Subsequently, 30 μL of Fe-LCDs (1 mg/mL), 20 μL of TMB (50 mM), and 10 μL of H2O2 (1 mM) were added to the HAc/NaAc buffer and reacted for 5 min at 25 °C. The color change of the reaction solution was monitored, and the absorbance change at 652 nm of oxTMB was recorded using UV–Vis spectroscopy. Four experimental groups were established with the following final concentrations: (1) TMB alone (1 mM), (2) TMB (1 mM) + H₂O₂ (0.01 mM), (3) TMB (1 mM) + Fe-LCDs (30 μg/mL), and (4) TMB (1 mM) + H₂O₂ (0.01 mM) + Fe-LCDs (30 μg/mL). In addition, the effects of pH (1.0–8.0), reaction temperature (20–70 °C), and reaction times (1–14 min) on the peroxidase-like activity were assessed using the same methodology.

To elucidate the mechanism underlying the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) by Fe-LCDs during the catalytic decomposition of H2O2, ROS scavenging experiments were performed in the presence of peroxidase substrate TMB. Isopropanol (IPA), benzoquinone (BQ), and L-histidine (His) were selected as scavengers for hydroxyl radicals (·OH), superoxide anions (O2 ·-), and singlet oxygen (1O2), respectively (Bi et al., 2021). In a HAc/NaAc buffer (0.2 M, pH = 3.5) containing TMB, H2O2, and Fe-LCDs, specific concentrations of IPA, BQ, and His were added to the solution, respectively. The reaction was carried out at 25 °C for 5 min, and the absorption spectra of the mixed solutions were recorded using UV–Vis spectroscopy.

In addition, 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline-N-oxide (DMPO) was utilized as a spin-trapping agent for the further detection of O2 ·- (Li, Li, et al., 2022; Li, Liu, et al., 2022; Li, Wang, et al., 2022). DMPO was added to a HAc/NaAc buffer (0.2 M, pH = 3.5) containing both H2O2 and Fe-LCDs. The mixture was allowed to react at 25 °C for 5 min, and the production of O2 ·- was confirmed through electron spin resonance (ESR) spectroscopy.

To investigate the kinetics of enzymatic activity of Fe-LCDs, a consistent concentration of Fe-LCDs was added to NaAc/HAc buffer (0.2 M, pH = 3.5). The concentration of substrate TMB was maintained, while the concentration of H2O2 varied from 0.04 to 0.18 mM. The absorbance at 652 nm was monitored over time. Subsequently, the concentration of H2O2 was fixed, and the concentration of TMB was altered from 0.1 to 1.4 mM, with the absorbance at 652 nm recorded continuously (Li, Li, et al., 2022; Li, Liu, et al., 2022; Li, Wang, et al., 2022). Finally, the reaction rate and substrate concentration were fitted to the Michaelis-Menten equation (Jiang et al., 2018), and the kinetic constants V max and K m were calculated by:

where V represents the initial reaction velocity (in ms−1), K m denotes the Michaelis constant (in mM), [S] signifies the substrate concentration (in mM), and V max indicates maximal reaction velocity (in ms−1).

CRH catalyzes the conversion of CR to sarcosine (SAR) and urea, while SOx facilitates the hydrolysis of SAR, resulting in the production of H2O2. In turn, Fe-LCDs catalyze the generation of ROS from H2O2, which oxidizes colorless TMB to blue oxTMB (Wu et al., 2019). Consequently, the concentration of CR can be quantified by observing the color change associated with the formation of oxTMB and measuring its absorbance at 652 nm. Briefly, 100 μL of CR at fixed concentrations (1–50 μM), 50 μL of CRH (15 U/mL), and 50 μL of SOx (15 U/mL) were first combined in 150 μL of PBS buffer (10 mM, pH = 7.4) and reacted at 37 °C for 30 min. The solution was then transferred to a NaAc/HAc buffer (0.2 M, pH = 3.5), supplemented with 60 μL of Fe-LCDs (1 mg/mL) and 20 μL of TMB (50 mM) at a total volume of 2 mL (Lan et al., 2014). After allowing it to proceed at 25 °C for 5 min, the absorbance of the mixed solution at 652 nm was recorded using UV–Vis spectroscopy.

To realize rapid testing, a smartphone-assisted was employed for the detection of CR, as demonstrated in previous studies (Chen et al., 2024; Gaona-Ruiz et al., 2024). Initially, CR was subjected to the above colorimetric detection procedure and the color of the reaction solution was captured using the smartphone's camera. The color parameters were then analyzed using the publicly available “Color Identification” smartphone application, enabling the establishment of a linear relationship between these parameters and the sample concentration, thereby permitting the quantitative detection of CR. All the images were taken by a smartphone OPPO Reno 12 (model: PJV110) features a 50-megapixel camera.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of CR in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the feasibility of colorimetric detection and smartphone imaging sensors, duck, beef, pork, lamb, and chicken samples were randomly purchased from local food markets and restaurants, with each sample originating from distinct animal individuals. The meat samples were first extracted following the method outlined by a previous protocol (Mora et al., 2007), with slight modifications. Specifically, 1.0 g of each meat sample was homogenized into a puree, to which 9 mL of 0.01 M HCl and 1 mL of a specific concentration of trichloroacetic acid were added, followed by sonication for 30 min. After centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 20 min, the supernatant was neutralized to pH 7 using NaOH and subsequently filtered through a 0.22 μm needle filter to obtain the processed sample solution, which was stored at 4 °C for later use. The solution was diluted as necessary prior to testing. Both colorimetric detection and smartphone imaging sensors were employed to measure the concentration of CR in the samples with the testing.

Ultra-performance liquid chromatography (UPLC) was employed to validate the detection results, with CR quantification performed following the method of (Kim & Jang, 2021) with slight modifications. The CR was analyzed using a Thermo Syncronis HILIC column (100 × 2.1 mm, 1.7 μm; Thermo Scientific) maintained at 40 °C. The mobile phase comprised (A) 2 mM ammonium acetate in ultrapure water and (B) acetonitrile. A gradient elution program was employed as follows: initial 10 % A (0–1 min), linear increase to 40 % A (1–2 min), isocratic hold at 40 % A (2–4 min), linear decrease to 10 % A (4–4.5 min), and column re-equilibration at 10 % A (4.5–7 min). The mobile phase flow rate was maintained at 0.30 mL/min.

The morphology of iron-loaded carbon dots Fe-LCDs was investigated using transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As illustrated in Fig. 1a, the Fe-LCDs are mono-disperse spherical particles. The high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) image (in the inset of Fig. 1a) reveals a lattice structure with a spacing of 0.22 nm, which corresponds to the lattice spacing of the graphene (100) crystal plane (Lu et al., 2023). Additionally, the average diameter of the Fe-LCDs is approximately 2.19 ± 0.02 nm (Fig. 1b). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was employed to analyze the distribution of characteristic functional groups on the surface of Fe-LCDs. Fig. 1c illustrates the abundance of these functional groups. The peak at 3390 cm−1 corresponds to the OH stretching vibration, while the peaks at 1628 cm−1 and 1136 cm−1 are attributed to CO and CO bonds, respectively (Xu et al., 2022). Furthermore, compared with the FT-IR spectrum of the LCDs, the absorption peak at 599 cm−1 corresponds to the deformation vibration of FeC (Fig. S1), indicating successful elemental modification of Fe (Cui et al., 2023). Moreover, the composition and valence states of the elements in Fe-LCDs were further analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). As shown in Fig. 1d, the XPS displays five peaks at 168.85, 198.61, 284.8, 532.1, and 710.83 eV, indicating that the primary elements of Fe-LCDs are S, Cl, C, O, and Fe, respectively. The carbon element constitutes 55.61 % of the total, whereas O, S, Fe, and Cl account for 33.65 %, 5.24 %, 3.37 %, and 2.13 %, respectively (Table S1). The C1s spectrum shows four peaks near 284.8, 286.4, 288.3, and 289.1 eV, corresponding to C-C/C=C, CO, CCl, and CO (Fig. S2a) (Chandra et al., 2019). The O1s spectrum exhibits two peaks at 531.2 and 532.0 eV, representing CO and CO (Fig. S2b) (Dong et al., 2025). The S2p spectrum (Fig. S2c) displays peaks at 167.9 and 168.9 eV, corresponding to S 2p₃/₂ and S 2p₁/₂. The high-resolution XPS spectra of Fe 2p (Fig. 1e) and Cl 2p (Fig. 1f) align well with the theoretical predictions for Fe 2p₁/₂, Fe 2p₃/₂, Cl 2p₁/₂, and Cl 2p₃/₂, suggesting that Fe-LCDs contain both Fe (II) and Fe (III) (Cao et al., 2023; Yin et al., 2024). The results from XPS and FT-IR are consistent, further confirming the successful doping of Fe. Additionally, the optical properties of Fe-LCDs were briefly characterized. As illustrated in Fig. S3, the optimal excitation and emission wavelengths of Fe-LCDs are 309 nm and 378 nm, respectively.

The nanozyme activity of Fe-LCDs was investigated through colorimetric experiments, in which their capability to catalyze the conversion of H2O2 into ROS was evaluated using TMB as a peroxidase substrate. Fig. 2a shows that, in the absence of Fe-LCDs, neither TMB nor TMB + H2O2 solutions showed color changes and had no significant absorption peaks. While the addition of Fe-LCDs to the colorless TMB-H2O2 solution resulted in the transformation of TMB into the blue oxTMB, with a maximum absorbance at 652 nm, confirming the peroxidase-like activity of Fe-LCDs (Ma et al., 2021). Similarly, the activity was further verified utilizing other peroxidase substrates, specifically 2,2′-azino-bis (3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid) (ABTS) and o-phenylenediamine (OPD). As shown in Fig. S4, ABTS changed from colorless to green, displaying absorption peaks at 405 nm and 734 nm. Furthermore, OPD transitioned from colorless to yellow, exhibiting an absorption peak at 450 nm (Bi et al., 2021). These results further demonstrate that the Fe-LCDs possess inherent peroxidase-mimicking activity.

Iron doping plays a key role in enhancing the catalytic performance, stability, and versatility of nanozymes, which makes it highly attractive for applications in biosensing and analytical chemistry (Wu et al., 2024). To investigate the effectiveness of iron doping in Fe-LCDs, lignosulfonate carbon dots (LCDs) were synthesized using sodium lignosulfonate as the sole precursor via the same synthetic method. Fe-LCDs and LCDs were reacted with H2O2 under identical conditions for 5 min, respectively, and the absorbance at 652 nm was measured. As illustrated in Fig. 2b, Fe-LCDs exhibited more vigorous peroxidase-like activity than the LCDs, demonstrating that iron doping was critical for the significantly enhanced peroxidase-like activity of Fe-LCDs.

Similar to natural horseradish peroxidase (HRP), the enzymatic activity of Fe-LCDs may be influenced by various environmental conditions. Therefore, the effects of pH (1.0–8.0), temperature (20–60 °C), and reaction time (0–14 min) on the enzymatic activity of Fe-LCDs were investigated. Fig. 2c showed that the enzymatic activity increased with pH from 1.0 to 3.5 and then decreased as pH further increased to 8.0, with the maximum activity observed at pH 3.5. Moreover, the relative activity increased gradually with the temperature and reached a plateau at 50 °C (Fig. 2d), demonstrating remarkable thermal stability that surpasses most reported nanozyme systems. This optimal temperature performance contrasts sharply with conventional Fe₃O₄ nanozymes, which typically exhibit significant activity decay above 40 °C due to nanoparticle aggregation (Gao et al., 2007). Notably, the enzymatic reaction of Fe-LCDs exhibited remarkably fast kinetics, approaching completion within merely 5 min (Fig. S5), demonstrating a substantial time advantage over conventional nanozyme systems (Liu, Qiao, et al., 2022; Liu, Xu, et al., 2022). Considering the practical applications and requirements of the colorimetric assay, the optimal conditions for the peroxidase-like activity of Fe-LCDs-based nanozymes were determined to be a reaction at 25 °C for 5 min in a buffer solution with a pH of 3.5.

To elucidate the catalytic mechanism of Fe-LCDs nanozymes, we evaluated the type of ROS generated during the reaction through free radical scavenger quenching experiments. His, IPA, and BQ were employed to scavenge 1O2, ·OH, and O2 ·-, respectively (Yang et al., 2024). According to Fig. 2e and Fig. S6, the addition of BQ significantly reduced the peroxidase-like activity of Fe-LCDs compared to the control group, while treatments with His and IPA exhibited negligible changes relative to the control. These results indicate that O2 ·- is the primary ROS involved in the catalytic oxidation of H2O2 by Fe-LCDs nanozymes, whereas ·OH and 1O2 contribute minimally. Additionally, electron spin resonance (ESR) experiments were utilized to corroborate these findings using 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline-N-oxide (DMPO) as the trapping agent. As shown in Fig. 2f, no ESR signal was detected in the absence of Fe-LCDs, while four strong characteristic peaks with an intensity ratio of 1:1:1:1 appeared in the DMPO+H2O2 + Fe-LCDs group, further validating the production of O2 ·-. These findings suggested that the peroxidase-like activity of Fe-LCDs chiefly originated from the generation of the active intermediate O2 ·-, which further oxidized TMB to produce colorimetric signals (Jiang et al., 2024).

Steady-state kinetic analysis was performed to explore the catalytic behavior of Fe-LCDs by using TMB and H2O2 as substrates. As shown in Fig. 3 , the catalytic efficacy of Fe-LCDs varied with the increasing concentrations of both TMB and H2O2. To determine the kinetic parameters, Lineweaver-Burk plots were constructed using 1/[S] and 1/[v] as the horizontal and vertical coordinates, respectively. The corresponding K m and V max of TMB and H2O2 were derived from the intercepts and slopes of the fitted double-reciprocal lines to represent the affinity of the nanozyme toward the substrate and the catalytic velocity of the enzymatic reaction, respectively. As listed in Table S2, the Fe-LCDs exhibited superior affinity for TMB and H2O2, with K m of 0.444 mM and 0.086 mM, respectively. This performance surpassed natural HRP and conventional artificial peroxidases such as Fe3O4-based nanozymes (Gao et al., 2007). Additionally, the substrate affinity of Fe-LCDs stands out among most CDs-based nanozymes synthesized from similar materials, likely due to their unique surface structural and electronic properties. Furthermore, Fe-LCDs demonstrated the V max values of 8.12 × 10−7 ms−1 for TMB and 8.31 × 10−7 ms−1 for H2O2, which are notably higher than those of most other peroxidases. This enhanced catalytic performance of Fe-LCDs may be attributed to the synergistic effect of sodium lignosulfonate and iron doping. These findings not only highlight the superiority of Fe-LCDs over existing peroxidases but also provide insights into the rational design of high-performance nanozymes for catalytic applications.

The potential of the Fe-LCDs catalytic system in the detection of CR was explored. Fe-LCDs with peroxidase-like activity can catalyze the conversion of H2O2 to O2 ·- and facilitate the oxidation of TMB to oxTMB, resulting in a characteristic absorption peak at 652 nm and imparting a blue color to the solution (Wu et al., 2019). Importantly, CR can be sequentially catalyzed by CRH and SOx to produce glycine, methanol, and H2O2. The generated H2O2 provides the necessary substrate for the enzyme-activated catalytic process involving Fe-LCDs. Based on these principles, a simple and cost-effective colorimetric sensing platform using Fe-LCDs-based nanozymes for the detection of CR was developed. The reaction process of the enzyme-nanozyme cascade is illustrated as follows (Lan et al., 2014; Zhu et al., 2022):
Creatine + H 2 O
→ CRH
Sarcosine + Urea
Sarcosine + H 2 O + O 2
→ SOx
Glycine + Methanol + H 2 O 2
TMB + H 2 O 2
→
Fe − LCDs
oxTMB + H 2 O

In Fig. 4a, within the HAc/NaAc buffer system containing Fe-LCDs and TMB, the absorbance at 652 nm was negligibly affected when SOx was present with either CR or CRH alone. In contrast, the addition of both CR and CRH resulted in a significant increase in absorbance at 652 nm, accompanied by a noticeable change in the solution color to blue. This phenomenon was attributed to the catalytic hydrolysis of CR by the combined action of CRH and SOx, which generated H2O2 and subsequently promoted the oxidation of TMB. Fig. 4b displays the absorbance curves corresponding to various concentrations of CR from 0 to 50 μM. As the concentration of CR increases, the production of H2O2 rises correspondingly, leading to a deepening of the blue color and an increase in absorbance. A significant linear relationship was observed between absorbance at 652 nm and CR concentration within the range of 1–50 μM, with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.09 μM, as illustrated in Fig. 4c. As shown in Table S3, the Fe-LCDs-based colorimetric sensing platform demonstrates superior sensitivity for CR detection compared to conventional analytical methods (Shen et al., 2024; Surucu & Abaci, 2022). This enhanced sensitivity enables reliable CR quantification in complex meat matrices at low concentrations.

The inherent specificity of nanozymes could effectively address interference from meat interfering constituents, which are susceptible to coelution with CR during HPLC analysis, potentially affecting the precision and reliability of the detection outcomes (Feizi et al., 2022). To address the potential complexity in actual samples, selective testing was conducted to identify possible interfering substances in the detection of CR. As shown in Fig. 4d, the assay system included various substances, such as L-leucine, L-arginine, L-glutamic acid, lysine, aspartic acid, vitamin B12, vitamin B6, Na+、K+、Zn2+、Mg2+、Ca2+、Fe3+、Cl−、NO3 −、SO4 2−、CO3 2− and PO4 3−. Fortunately, these interfering substances did not cause any changes to the solution color or absorbance, thereby confirming the method's excellent specificity and selectivity (Huang et al., 2023). The high selectivity of the Fe-LCDs-based sensing platform can be primarily attributed to the specific catalytic ability of CRH and SOx for CR. Meanwhile, the stability of the peroxidase-like activity of Fe-LCDs nanozymes was evaluated both in solid powder and water solution forms. Fig. 4e demonstrates that the Fe-LCDs nanozyme solid powder can be stored for 24 weeks or longer while maintaining stable enzymatic activity. Additionally, the Fe-LCDs dispersed in ultrapure water and NaAc buffer do not exhibit precipitation or aggregation over time, and their enzymatic activity remains consistently stable for up to 12 days (Fig. 4f and Fig. S7). Therefore, the CR sensing platform based on Fe-LCDs exhibits excellent selectivity and storage stability, indicating its potential for application in practical samples.

A smartphone was integrated into the colorimetric sensing platform based on Fe-LCDs nanozymes to realize rapid detection of CR. First, colorimetric images of solutions with varying CR concentrations were captured using smartphones. Then, the colors in the images were identified by the “Color Identification” smartphone application, which converted them into Hue (H), Saturation (S), and Lightness (L) values (Fig. 5a) (Lou-Franco et al., 2023). The S values were plotted on the ordinate. In contrast, the concentration of CR was represented on the abscissa. As displayed in Fig. 5b, a strong linear relationship was observed in the range of 1–50 μM and with a LOD of 0.95 μM. While the LOD is higher than that of our colorimetric sensing platform, it remains lower than those of conventional analytical methods (Table S3) (Feizi et al., 2022; Surucu & Abaci, 2022). Furthermore, the smartphone-assisted sensing platform operates without specialized instrumentation, enabling direct CR quantification via smartphone. This combination of high sensitivity, cost-effectiveness, and operational simplicity demonstrates a strong potential for food safety monitoring.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of CR in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To demonstrate the validity and reliability of the developed method, duck, beef, pork, lamb, and chicken samples were randomly purchased from local food markets (referred to as Sample 1 in each group) and restaurants (referred to as Sample 2 in each group), with each sample originating from distinct animal individuals. We first extracted CR from meat samples and the purification steps included centrifugation and filtration to remove large particles from meat juice, followed by protein precipitation using trichloroacetic acid to obtain a clarified extract containing dissolved CR (Mora et al., 2007). Both the colorimetric detection method and the smartphone-assisted sensing platform were employed to determine the concentrations of CR in the meat samples. As shown in Fig. 5c, the results from these two analytical approaches exhibited a high degree of concordance. The high interference resistance of this method stems from the exceptional substrate specificity of creatinase and sarcosine oxidase, which effectively eliminate potential interference from other soluble components in the extract while ensuring accurate CR quantification. Interestingly, the content of CR in meat samples from restaurants was lower than that in meat purchased from food markets, highlighting the effectiveness of the developed methods in elucidating the heterogeneity in meat quality across different sources. To further validate the accuracy of the developed assay, the same samples were analyzed using UPLC techniques (Fig. S8) (Kim & Jang, 2021). The CR content derived from UPLC was in close agreement with the results obtained from colorimetric detection and smartphone-assisted monitoring, with relative standard deviation (RSD) ranging from 2.18 to 6.17 % (Fig. 5c). These findings indicate that the Fe-LCDs nanozyme-based analytical strategies exhibit high accuracy, robustness, and reliability for detecting CR in meat products, thereby providing advantages for commercialization and scalability in future applications.

In summary, this study successfully developed a smartphone-assisted colorimetric sensing platform based on Fe-LCDs nanozymes for CR detection in meat. Fe-LCDs-based nanozymes with high peroxidase-like activity were prepared via a cost-effective, simple, and one-pot method. By integrating enzyme and nanozyme sensing cascade, CR undergoes sequential oxidation by CRH and SOx to produce H2O2, which was further catalyzed by the Fe-LCDs nanozyme to generate free radicals O2 ·-. This process facilitates the oxidation of the substrate TMB into blue oxTMB, thereby enabling colorimetric detection of CR. This Fe-LCDs-based colorimetric platform presented high sensitivity for the detection of CR, achieving a low LOD of 0.09 μM. Furthermore, the smartphone-based sensing platform enables rapid and visual detection of CR in meat products, with an LOD of 0.95 μM. The developed method exhibits improved sensitivity over conventional techniques. Notably, the proposed strategy demonstrated excellent selectivity and accuracy in actual meat samples, as validated by UPLC technology. Consequently, a simple Fe-LCDs-based platform was developed for rapid, sensitive CR detection in meat, showing promise for food quality monitoring. However, challenges persist in transitioning portable detection systems from laboratory-dependent sample preparation to practical on-site applications. Future research should prioritize developing scalable solutions for field-deployable detection, such as integrating paper-based substrates or patches to enable direct, real-time evaluation of CR content in meat. Additionally, efforts should focus on incorporating machine learning algorithms to enhance the accuracy and reliability of smartphone-based data interpretation, thereby enabling more robust and user-friendly analytical tools for on-site meat quality assessment.
